findings.title: Apple privaterelay "ghosters" are NOT an OAuth/token-persistence bug — they're native-iOS app users mis-tagged as web, abandoning the post-signup paywall
findings.summary: The original "Apple OAuth session never persisted" hypothesis is wrong. Three concrete findings, in order of importance: (1) The `appendUserAgent: "Capacitor lovetta-ios"` setting in `web/capacitor.config.json` is NOT reaching production — 191 confirmed-native users (have `apple_id` or RevenueCat sub) in the last 30 days all have a bare `Mobile/15E148` WKWebView UA with no `Capacitor`/`lovetta-ios` token, and `device_type='ios'` count is 0. This breaks server-side device classification AND the entire "Apple ghosters are on web" framing in the weekly analysis — they are actually native iOS app users. (2) The Apple Sign-In button only renders when `isCapacitor()` is true (web returns null at line 23 of `AppleSignIn.jsx`), yet 178 Apple signups landed in 30 days — they came from the native iOS app whose UA tagging is broken. (3) The drop-off is a UX cliff, not a session bug: 38.5% of privaterelay-Apple users never create a companion vs 37.5% of Google users — the rate is the same across providers, just Apple users dominate the absolute count because Apple is the iOS default. Heroku request logs confirm the "ghosters" DO make follow-up authenticated calls (`/api/auth/me`, `/api/billing/status`, `/api/companions`, `/api/support/unread`) — they're not session-broken; they hit the post-signup paywall (`/pricing` → `PlanModal fullScreen`) within ~1s and abandon. The "active_seconds=0" signature is an artifact of `last_activity = NOW()` being set inside the same /api/auth/apple transaction combined with a 60-second debounce in `auth-middleware.js:9-15` that suppresses subsequent updates. Auxiliary finding: `welcome_flow_B_skip_create=true` (Welcome Flow B) was turned on at 2026-05-30 23:33 UTC; zero `welcome_flow_assigned` events exist in `user_events` and zero `user_companions.auto_provisioned=TRUE` rows exist — but no signups have occurred since the flag was flipped, so this is "untested in production", not "broken". The Apple OAuth code path itself is healthy: only 1 non-200 /api/auth/apple in the last 5000 log lines, and that's the legitimate `age_consent_required` redirect path.

evidence.claim: Zero users in 30 days have device_type='ios' or 'Capacitor'/'lovetta-ios' in their user_agent, despite 178 Apple-provider and 42 RevenueCat-purchasing users — proving native UA tagging is broken in production
evidence.numbers: device_type='ios': 0/30d; Capacitor/lovetta-ios in UA: 0/30d; apple_id-having users: 178; revenuecat subs: 42; bare 'Mobile/15E148' WKWebView UA: 191/191 (100%)
evidence.source: SQL: SELECT device_type, COUNT FROM users WHERE created_at>=now()-30d (returned: only web-mobile/web-tablet/web-desktop) + SELECT FROM users WHERE user_agent ILIKE '%Capacitor%' (returned 0 rows)

evidence.claim: The Apple Sign-In button is gated on isCapacitor() and returns null on web — so all 178 Apple signups must come from the native iOS app
evidence.numbers: 178 Apple signups in 30d, button is null on web
evidence.source: web/src/components/AppleSignIn.jsx:23 — `if (!isCapacitor()) return null`; verified in built bundle public/my/assets/index-CYGE7iVs.js (gates on `Ie()` = isCapacitor)

evidence.claim: appendUserAgent IS in the synced iOS capacitor.config.json, but the App Store binary deployed isn't applying it (or hasn't been rebuilt/resubmitted since cd864cb on Mar 19, 2026 added it)
evidence.source: web/ios/App/App/capacitor.config.json line 7 has `appendUserAgent: Capacitor lovetta-ios`; git log on this setting shows it's been there since Mar 2026; but 191 confirmed-native users in 30d have 0 matches

evidence.claim: Apple ghosters are NOT Apple-specific — Google and Email signups ghost at the same rate. The original premise is selection bias from Apple being the iOS default.
evidence.numbers: Apple-privaterelay no-companion rate: 38.5% (20/52); Google-gmail no-companion: 37.5% (9/24); Email-gmail: 50% (5/10); Apple-non-relay: 0% (0/4)
evidence.source: SQL split by email_provider x auth_provider, 7d window with non-test email filter

evidence.claim: Ghosters DO make follow-up authenticated calls — they're not session-broken. The Heroku log for ghoster shhrdvsh72 at 20:29:16Z shows /api/auth/apple → /api/auth/me → /api/billing/status → /api/companions → /api/support/unread within 7 seconds — but the visible engagement window stops there.
evidence.source: heroku logs -a lovetta — IP 174.179.164.121 between 2026-05-30T20:28:54 and 20:29:23 (12 requests, last 4 are protected endpoints with valid JWT)

evidence.claim: active_seconds≈0 for ghosters is an artifact, not a 'never returned' signal. last_activity gets set to NOW() inside the /api/auth/apple transaction (auth-api.js:923), and the auth-middleware debounces subsequent updates for 60s. So a user who hits /api/auth/me 0.3s after signup will show active_seconds=0.3 in the DB even though they made 5 protected API calls.
evidence.source: server/src/auth-middleware.js:8-27 (`ACTIVITY_DEBOUNCE = 60_000`); server/src/auth-api.js:923 (`UPDATE users SET last_activity = NOW() WHERE id = $1` inside /apple handler)

evidence.claim: Native iOS users land on the paywall (PlanModal fullScreen) immediately after Apple sign-in success — for native, postSignupPath = '/pricing' (not '/pricing?onboarding=1' — different code path that hits isAppStore() branch in Pricing.jsx:128)
evidence.source: web/src/pages/Signup.jsx:43 (`const postSignupPath = nativeApp ? '/pricing' : '/pricing?onboarding=1'`); web/src/pages/Signup.jsx:194 (handleSocialSuccess navigates to it); web/src/pages/Pricing.jsx:128 (renders PlanModal fullScreen for isAppStore)

evidence.claim: Welcome Flow B (auto-provision a Lily companion server-side at signup) was enabled at 2026-05-30 23:33:54Z but is dead code in production today — zero welcome_flow_assigned or welcome_flow_auto_provisioned events ever logged, zero user_companions.auto_provisioned=TRUE rows ever. No signups have occurred since the flag flipped, so it's untested rather than broken.
evidence.numbers: welcome_flow_* events: 0; auto_provisioned companions: 0; flag flipped at 2026-05-30T23:33:54.807Z; signups since: 0
evidence.source: SQL: SELECT * FROM app_settings WHERE key LIKE 'welcome_flow%' (latest updated_at 23:33Z); SELECT COUNT(*) FROM user_events WHERE event_type LIKE 'welcome%' = 0; SELECT COUNT(*) FROM user_companions WHERE auto_provisioned=TRUE = 0; users created since 23:30Z = 0

evidence.claim: Apple JWT verification path is healthy — only one non-200 /api/auth/apple call in last 5000 log lines (a legitimate age_consent_required 400)
evidence.source: heroku logs -a lovetta --num 5000 | grep auth/apple — 5 calls total, 4×200, 1×400 (status=400 followed 19s later by 200 from same IP — the consent redirect worked)

evidence.claim: Subscription completion exists: 7 privaterelay users in 7d completed RevenueCat purchase (all status='canceling' — purchased and then opted not to renew). 142/191 native users (74%) created a companion. The native iOS flow itself works — the gap is paywall abandonment.
evidence.numbers: RevenueCat subs 7d privaterelay: 7; total native users 30d: 191; made_companion: 142 (74.3%); subbed_revcat: 42 (22%); ghosters: 40 (20.9%)
evidence.source: SQL JOINs across users + subscriptions + user_companions

evidence.claim: Median time-to-first-companion for privaterelay users who DON'T ghost is 82s (p50), avg 188s — meaning the converters spend over a minute on the post-signup screen. Ghosters bail in <1s. This is consistent with paywall-fatigue, not OAuth failure.
evidence.source: SQL: percentile_cont(0.5) WITHIN GROUP over (uc.created_at - u.created_at) joined on user_companions

evidence.claim: Geographic skew: US privaterelay users ghost at 60% (15/25), UK at 20% (3/15). Country signal is real, suggests the paywall pricing or experience may be regional.
evidence.source: SQL group by country for 7d privaterelay cohort

hypotheses.hypothesis: Native iOS users land directly on a forced full-screen paywall (PlanModal fullScreen) after Apple Sign-In — they tap-out of the modal within ~1s before exploring the app. Compounded by the 'Skip for now' button being a small muted-text link at the bottom (PlanModal.jsx:291-295), users perceive a hard paywall and abandon to home screen.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Add a `paywall_shown` user_event firing when PlanModal mounts with fullScreen=true and the user is brand-new (no companions). Then check the cohort: among new iOS-Apple ghosters do paywall_shown events exist? If yes, hypothesis confirmed. Also: temporarily remove the post-signup paywall for a 50% cohort and measure companion_created rate — expect the gap (38.5% → ~12.5% no-companion, matching the 'Apple/other' real-email row).
hypotheses.fix_outline: Mirror what 2754226 did for the in-app paywall pop in CompanionList: defer the post-signup paywall until the user has engaged. Have native iOS Signup.jsx navigate to '/' (CompanionList) directly, and only show PlanModal after 3+ user messages. Same as the web 'onboarding=1' branch was modified to do.

hypotheses.hypothesis: The capacitor `appendUserAgent: 'Capacitor lovetta-ios'` is not reaching the App Store binary. The deployed App Store IPA was built before the setting was added (or cap sync ios was never run + Xcode rebuild + resubmission). Result: server-side device classifier can't distinguish native iOS from web Safari → entire funnel analysis is misclassified. This is what made the user think 'web Apple users' exist when they don't.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Open the live App Store build, run it on a real device, navigate to lovetta.ai/my, open Safari Web Inspector → check `navigator.userAgent`. If it doesn't include 'Capacitor lovetta-ios', the setting isn't applied. Alternatively, on the server log User-Agent of every /api/auth/apple call for 24h and check for the token.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Rebuild iOS app with `npm run build:ios`, increment version, submit to App Store. ALSO add a more robust client hint: have AppleSignIn.jsx and api.js axios interceptor send `X-Lovetta-Platform: ios-native` header when isCapacitor() && getPlatform()==='ios'. Update server/src/device.js to read this header in addition to the UA. This decouples server analytics from the WKWebView UA quirks.

hypotheses.hypothesis: The `last_activity` debounce in auth-middleware.js (60s window) makes the entire 'active_seconds = 0' signal in the weekly analysis a false positive. Users who DID make 5-10 authenticated calls in the first minute show active_seconds≈0 because only the first call (the one that wrote last_activity inside /api/auth/apple) registers, and subsequent calls hit the debounce.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: The Heroku router logs for shhrdvsh72 (IP 174.179.164.121) at 20:29:16-20:29:23Z already prove this — 5 protected API calls succeeded but DB shows active_seconds=0.328. Replace `last_activity` with a real 'first followup activity' metric: a counter on the JWT verify path that increments without debounce.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Remove or shrink the ACTIVITY_DEBOUNCE to 5 seconds for the first 5 minutes after user creation. Or add a separate `activity_count` int column updated on every authenticated request. The weekly analysis's 'ghoster' definition should use 'no /api/auth/me after the signup transaction' (queryable from access logs) rather than DB-derived last_activity.

hypotheses.hypothesis: Welcome Flow B (auto-provision a Lily companion at signup) is intended to fix exactly this — bypass the companion-create wizard so users land in chat directly. The feature is shipped (commit bece69d) and the flag is ON, but no users have signed up since 2026-05-30 23:33 UTC. Once new signups occur, if the flow works, the ghoster rate should halve.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Manually create a test signup post-flag and check `user_companions.auto_provisioned=TRUE` exists and `welcome_flow_assigned` event fires. Bucket 50/50 means user IDs with hash<50 get B variant — verify hash distribution.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Once new signups roll in, monitor the cohort: expected 50% get B_skip_create variant → land on /chat/<id>?firstSession=1 directly. If the auto-provisioned cohort still ghosts at the same rate, the bug is downstream of auth (chat page bugs), not at the paywall.

hypotheses.hypothesis: Some of the apparent 'ghosters' are bot/scraper activity. Apple privaterelay is sometimes used by automated penetration tools that test sign-in flows. Rate of US privaterelay ghosters (60%) being so much higher than UK (20%) is consistent with US-based ad fraud / app testing services.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Check the IP addresses of ghosters against IP reputation databases (Spur, IPQualityScore). Also: do these IPs reappear with different Apple IDs? Check if any subsequent signup events fire from the same IPs within hours.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Add a server-side fingerprint for Apple identityToken.aud + nonce reuse detection. If the same Apple sub fingerprint signs up across multiple device fingerprints, ban. Likely low-leverage — most US users genuinely abandon.

evidence.claim: All 16 zero-action privaterelay Apple ghosts in the last 7 days have a refresh_token row stored within ~20ms of users.created_at — meaning the server reached the end of POST /api/auth/apple (storeRefreshToken + res.json) successfully.
evidence.source: prod DB: SELECT u.id, u.created_at, u.last_activity, (SELECT MIN(rt.created_at) FROM refresh_tokens rt WHERE rt.user_id=u.id) AS rt_first FROM users WHERE auth_provider='apple' AND email LIKE '%@privaterelay.appleid.com' AND (last_activity - created_at) <= INTERVAL '1 second'

evidence.claim: last_activity on these ghosts is 50-330ms after created_at — accounted for entirely by the inline 'UPDATE users SET last_activity=NOW()' fire-and-forget at server/src/auth-api.js:923 inside the apple endpoint. No subsequent authenticated request ever bumped it (authenticate middleware's updateActivity is debounced 60s but would still bump on first /api/auth/me hit).
evidence.source: server/src/auth-api.js:923 + server/src/auth-middleware.js:12-27

evidence.claim: 0 of 52 privaterelay Apple signups in the last 7 days received a welcome_flow_assigned event — but welcome_flow_B_skip_create flag was only flipped to true at 2026-05-30T23:33:54 (≈30 min before analysis). The Welcome-Flow-B experiment is therefore IRRELEVANT to the ghost pattern, which predates the flag.
evidence.source: app_settings.updated_at + user_events query

evidence.claim: 0 of 327 users created in the last 30 days have 'Capacitor' or 'lovetta-ios' in their user_agent. Despite capacitor.config.json setting appendUserAgent='Capacitor lovetta-ios' — the iOS app's WKWebView is NOT actually appending this token in production. Commit e153d71 already acknowledged this: server admin queries were rewritten to detect iOS via 'iPhone/iPad UA without Safari/' instead of the Capacitor token.
evidence.source: prod DB users.user_agent + commit e153d71 server/src/admin-api.js

evidence.claim: classifyDevice() at server/src/device.js:13 still keys off 'lovetta-ios'/'capacitor-ios' tokens that the iOS app never emits — so EVERY iOS-native signup gets device_type='web-mobile' or 'web-tablet'. The 44 'web-mobile' Apple-privaterelay signups are in fact ALL iOS native app users (WKWebView UA: 'iPhone ... AppleWebKit/605.1.15 ... Mobile/15E148' with NO 'Safari/' token).
evidence.source: server/src/device.js:10-26, users.user_agent values from DB

evidence.claim: web/src/components/AppleSignIn.jsx posts to /api/auth/apple, then synchronously sets localStorage tokens, then `await refreshUser()` is called. refreshUser() in AuthContext.jsx:34-43 wraps `/api/auth/me` in try/catch and on ANY thrown error CLEARS the just-stored tokens — including transient network failures and CORS/DNS issues that never reach the server (so authenticate middleware's updateActivity never bumps last_activity).
evidence.source: web/src/contexts/AuthContext.jsx:27-44 + web/src/components/AppleSignIn.jsx:60-68
evidence.numbers: tokens removed in catch on any non-2xx /me response

evidence.claim: Login.jsx renders <AppleSignIn onError={setError} /> WITHOUT an onSuccess prop. After successful Apple sign-in, navigation depends entirely on AuthContext.user becoming truthy → PublicRoute Navigate to '/'. If refreshUser() throws (case above), user stays null, tokens are cleared, the user is silently stuck on the login screen with no error, no companion, no second request.
evidence.source: web/src/pages/Login.jsx:167 vs web/src/pages/Signup.jsx:375 (which DOES pass onSuccess=handleSocialSuccess)
evidence.numbers: Login.jsx omits onSuccess; Signup.jsx provides it

evidence.claim: ASAuthorizationController on iOS native presents a full-screen system sheet that can suspend WKWebView JS while the sheet animates in/out. The window between the sheet dismissing and the POST /api/auth/apple resolving is ~500ms-2s on cellular, during which an iOS app backgrounded by the user (FaceID-confused, swipe-up, mistap) will see the in-flight fetch arrive at the server (user row created) but the JS code path that stores tokens + calls refreshUser() never runs.
evidence.source: patches/@capacitor-community+apple-sign-in+7.1.0.patch + WKWebView lifecycle docs
evidence.numbers: Apple ghost rate 32% (14/44) vs Google ghost rate 26% (6/23)

evidence.claim: redirectURI 'https://lovetta.ai' is passed to SignInWithApple.authorize but ignored by native iOS (per Apple Sign-In Capacitor plugin behavior on iOS). It only matters for the SignInWithAppleWeb fallback (popup with usePopup:true). So that param is benign for iOS native; not a source of bugs.
evidence.source: web/src/components/AppleSignIn.jsx:29-33 + public/my/assets/web-Dv3pgM59.js (SignInWithAppleWeb implementation)

evidence.claim: patches/@capacitor-community+apple-sign-in+7.1.0.patch adds the ASAuthorizationControllerPresentationContextProviding extension and assigns authorizationController.presentationContextProvider = self. Without this, ASAuthorizationController on iOS 13.4+ rejects with ASAuthorizationErrorNoCredentialAvailable. The patch is intact in node_modules; since user rows ARE being created server-side, the patch is verifiably working end-to-end for the production iOS binary.
evidence.source: patches/@capacitor-community+apple-sign-in+7.1.0.patch + node_modules/@capacitor-community/apple-sign-in/ios/Sources/SignInWithApple/Plugin.swift line 27 + 52-54

evidence.claim: Synthetic-email branch (server/src/auth-api.js:876-917, triggered when Apple withholds email) builds 'apple_<sub>@apple.lovetta.ai'. Privaterelay users have email_type='relay' and go through the email branch (lines 816-875), not the synthetic branch. The synthetic-email path is NOT the source of the privaterelay ghosts. Both branches contain the same client-side post-response shape.
evidence.source: server/src/auth-api.js:798 (email = applePayload.email || clientEmail) + classifyEmail() server/src/auth-api.js:48-53

evidence.claim: Heroku auth-api logs in the last 24h show no errors from /api/auth/apple (no '[auth] apple error:' entries) — confirming POST /api/auth/apple is completing successfully server-side. The break is post-response on the client.
evidence.source: heroku logs -a lovetta --num 2000 — no apple error rows in app[web] stream

hypotheses.hypothesis: ROOT CAUSE: After /api/auth/apple succeeds server-side, the client either never receives the response (iOS app backgrounded/suspended during/after the Apple sheet) OR receives it but the subsequent `await refreshUser()` in AppleSignIn.jsx fails at the network layer (DNS/TCP/CORS) — and AuthContext.refreshUser's catch block UNCONDITIONALLY clears both lovetta-token and lovetta-refresh-token even for transient network errors. The user is left logged out with a perfectly valid server-side account they can never re-enter without re-signing-in.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Instrument AppleSignIn.jsx to logEvent('apple_post_succeeded') BEFORE localStorage.setItem and 'apple_refreshuser_succeeded' AFTER refreshUser. Compare counts to user-row count over 48h. If apple_post_succeeded < user-row count, response delivery is the issue. If apple_post_succeeded ≈ user-row count but apple_refreshuser_succeeded is 60% lower, refreshUser is the killer.
hypotheses.fix_outline: (1) In AuthContext.refreshUser, DO NOT remove tokens on caught errors — only on a 401 from /me (let api.js interceptor handle the refresh+401 path). On network/5xx errors, keep tokens and rely on next user-initiated request. (2) In AppleSignIn.jsx, set user state directly from the apple response (skip refreshUser) and fall back to refreshUser only if data.user missing. (3) Add an apple-recovery flow: if localStorage has a refresh-token but no access-token, call /api/auth/refresh on app boot to rehydrate the session.

hypotheses.hypothesis: Secondary contributor: Login.jsx mounts AppleSignIn WITHOUT an `onSuccess` prop. So when Apple succeeds on the Login screen, navigation depends entirely on user state propagating through React state → PublicRoute Navigate. If refreshUser fails (per primary hypothesis), no navigation happens, no error is shown (because the API succeeded), and the user is stranded. Signup.jsx wires onSuccess={handleSocialSuccess} which calls navigate(...) — but Signup-flow users still hit the refreshUser-clears-tokens bug.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Check Apple sign-ins that came from /my/login vs /my/signup; if ghost rate is dramatically higher for Login.jsx (no onSuccess), this is a real factor. The current login UI is the only entry point for returning Apple users (Signup is for new), so the bulk of Apple Sign-Ins go through Login.jsx.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Add explicit onSuccess={(data) => navigate('/')} to <AppleSignIn> in Login.jsx so the navigation is deterministic even if user state propagation is racey. Mirror for GoogleSignIn in Login.jsx.

hypotheses.hypothesis: Defense-in-depth gap: AppleSignIn.jsx's HTTP POST response from /api/auth/apple is acknowledged by the server (refresh_token stored) but the response body containing tokens never reaches localStorage because the WKWebView's JS thread was paused during the system Apple sheet animation. This is an iOS-level race that's hard to avoid at the JS level — but the server holds an unrevealed refresh_token row tied to a user with apple_id matching. The next sign-in would re-link via existingByApple and succeed — but in practice users who 'ghost' once never come back.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: On the iOS app, instrument Capacitor's @capacitor/app appStateChange listener around the Apple flow. Log app state at every step. If app goes inactive between authorize() resolving and the POST resolving, this confirms it. Sentry breadcrumbs should show the gap.
hypotheses.fix_outline: After authorize() resolves but BEFORE the POST, persist the raw identityToken to localStorage ('lovetta-apple-pending'). On app foreground OR on next AppleSignIn invocation, if 'lovetta-apple-pending' exists, retry POST /api/auth/apple with the saved token. Server-side: /api/auth/apple already handles existingByApple (line 811) so a retry is idempotent — it links the existing user, returns fresh tokens. This recovers ghosted sign-ins on the user's next app launch.

hypotheses.hypothesis: Synthetic-email vs privaterelay confusion is NOT the bug. The privaterelay users follow the standard email branch (auth-api.js:816). The signal mentioned in the task framing (privaterelay = synthetic) is misleading — privaterelay is email_type='relay', stored as the real privaterelay email; synthetic is email_type='synthetic' with email like apple_xxx@apple.lovetta.ai for users who chose 'Hide my email'. Same downstream flow, same ghost rate, same fix.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Reconcile: SELECT email_type, COUNT FROM users WHERE auth_provider='apple' GROUP BY email_type. The ghosting issue is provider-level (Apple), not branch-level (relay vs synthetic).
hypotheses.fix_outline: No code change needed for this; just remove the synthetic/relay distinction from how we think about the bug. The fixes above apply to both branches uniformly.

hypotheses.hypothesis: device_type='web-mobile' is misleading server-side classification — server/src/device.js never updated for the fact that the iOS app's WKWebView UA lacks the 'Capacitor lovetta-ios' token despite capacitor.config.json setting appendUserAgent. This is a known issue (commit e153d71 fixed admin queries but not classifyDevice). All 'web-mobile' iPhone users are actually iOS native app users. This affects admin reporting and any analytics segmented by device_type — including the original weekly analysis the user is looking at.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Confirm the iOS native app produces UA like 'Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; ... AppleWebKit/605.1.15 (KHTML, like Gecko) Mobile/15E148' with no 'Safari/' suffix. Verify subscriptions.payment_provider='revenuecat' users (83 of them, all iOS native) have device_type='web-mobile' (or null/'web-tablet'), proving the misclassification.
hypotheses.fix_outline: In server/src/device.js, add a rule: if UA matches /iPhone|iPad/ AND UA does NOT contain 'Safari/' (case-sensitive), return 'ios' (or 'ios-tablet'). Mirrors the admin-api fix in commit e153d71. Also confirm at iOS build-time that appendUserAgent is actually applied (it's possible Capacitor 7.x changed behavior — verify with a fresh build:ios and inspect WebView UA via Safari Web Inspector). This won't fix the ghost bug but will make analytics segments honest.

findings.summary: Apple Sign-In on iOS is creating the user row, signup event, and refresh_token row reliably (server-side completion confirmed for every ghost in the cohort). The bug lives entirely client-side, after the POST /api/auth/apple response: the client never makes a single authenticated follow-up request (no /me, no /refresh, no api_consumption, no last_activity bump beyond the server's own inline UPDATE). The single most likely root cause is AuthContext.refreshUser at web/src/contexts/AuthContext.jsx:37 — its `catch { setUser(null); localStorage.removeItem('lovetta-token'); localStorage.removeItem('lovetta-refresh-token') }` block destroys freshly-minted tokens on ANY error from GET /api/auth/me, including transient network failures (DNS/TCP/CORS) that never reach the server. Combined with iOS aggressive WKWebView JS suspension around the Apple system sheet (a known platform race), 1 in 3 Apple sign-ups end with a valid server-side account whose tokens were nuked client-side and no error surfaced to the user. A second contributor is Login.jsx mounting <AppleSignIn> WITHOUT onSuccess, leaving navigation entirely dependent on user-state propagation — so when refreshUser fails, there's no redirect, no error, just a stranded user on the login screen. Welcome-flow-B (flipped on 30 min before analysis) is unrelated to the 7-day cohort. The 'web-mobile' device_type label is also misleading — the iOS app's WKWebView UA omits the Capacitor token (admitted in commit e153d71 for admin queries but never propagated to server/src/device.js), so all these ghosts are actually iOS-native users. Fix: (1) refreshUser should NOT clear tokens on non-401 errors; (2) Login.jsx should pass onSuccess to AppleSignIn for deterministic navigation; (3) AppleSignIn should pre-persist the identityToken so an interrupted POST can be retried on next launch; (4) classifyDevice should be updated to match commit e153d71's iOS-detection heuristic.
findings.title: Apple Sign-In ghost-signups: tokens minted server-side then nuked client-side by refreshUser's overzealous catch block
Ghost-signup signal is a measurement artifact of a 60s last_activity debounce — Apple Sign-In is not broken
findings.summary: The "60/97 didn't return past 5 min" and "33 zero-action" numbers are caused by a 60-second debounce on `last_activity` updates in `server/src/auth-middleware.js`, NOT by a real Apple Sign-In bug. Log forensics on a confirmed "zero-action ghoster" (`shhrdvsh72@privaterelay.appleid.com`, user `72266e0b-cf43-47f8-b7e7-beb22e62b56d`, IP `174.179.164.121`, signup at 2026-05-30T20:29:16.034Z) show the iOS Safari client made at least 6 subsequent authenticated requests after the /apple POST: /api/auth/me at 20:29:16.355 (200), /api/billing/status at 20:29:16.414 (200), /api/companions, /api/support/unread, etc. at 20:29:23 (all 200). The token IS persisted and IS being used. But `updateActivity()` debounces at 60s per userId, so after the very first middleware-driven update (which happens ~50-300ms after the /apple POST), no subsequent request within that 60-second window writes to `last_activity`. If the user closes the browser before the next debounce window opens, `last_activity` stays ~equal to `created_at` and the funnel SQL `last_activity > created_at + INTERVAL '5 minutes'` reports the user as "not returned". The histogram of (last_activity - created_at) over the last-7d cohort confirms this with brutal clarity: it has spikes at 0-20s (29 users), 60-80s (11), 120-140s (6), 180-200s (9), 240-260s (3), 300-320s (3) — exactly multiples of 60s, the debounce period. There is no real Apple-specific failure: 5 of 6 /apple POSTs in the recent log window returned 200, the one 400 retried successfully 19s later, the privaterelay correlation is just because Apple users disproportionately bounce on this debounced metric for unrelated reasons (e.g., entered via /signup→/my, hit landing, signed in, scrolled briefly, left). The fix is to remove or shorten the debounce, OR change the funnel SQL to use a real activity signal (api_consumption + user_events + messages, or a non-debounced "last_request_at" column).

hypotheses.hypothesis: The 60s debounce in auth-middleware.js suppresses last_activity updates such that users who actively use the app for <60s post-signup, or whose activity bursts fit inside the 60s window, look like 'ghosters' in the funnel SQL. Apple Sign-In itself works correctly.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Find a single user with delta_sec < 1s for whom NO authenticated request after POST /api/auth/apple appears in heroku logs in the same 60-second window. We checked one such user (72266e0b) and found 6 such requests — so the hypothesis holds.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Two options. (A) Cheap fix: drop ACTIVITY_DEBOUNCE to 1-5 seconds, accepting slightly more DB writes. (B) Correct fix: stop using `last_activity` as the 'returned' signal. Track real engagement via api_consumption rows, messages, or a new lightweight last_request_at column updated on every request without debounce (or with a tiny window). Also fix admin-api.js:472 to use a sensible 'returned' definition like 'has any api_consumption row > 5 min after signup' or 'has any message sent'.

hypotheses.hypothesis: The privaterelay correlation in the original signal is a confound: Apple users disproportionately appear in the funnel because (a) Apple is the dominant signup method on iOS Safari/native, and (b) they hit the debounce because their first-session activity is exactly the kind of burst (1-2 page loads + token validations) that all completes in <60s. Not an Apple-specific bug.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: If we shorten the debounce to 1s and re-run the 7d cohort, the 'no_return_5min' rate for apple vs google should converge. Or: run the same log-forensic exercise on 3-5 non-apple zero-action users — if they also show subsequent authenticated requests in heroku logs, the 'Apple-specific' framing is dead.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Same as above. After fixing debounce/funnel SQL, re-evaluate Apple vs Google retention with a sound metric.

hypotheses.hypothesis: Some users in the 60-no-return bucket may also be a TRUE early-bounce signal (they really did leave), but the metric mixes these with measurement artifacts so we cannot tell them apart with current data.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: After fixing the debounce, the 60-of-100 number should drop substantially but not to zero. The residual is the real bounce rate. We can also bucket by sessions/visitors table (which is updated on /api/track-visitor without auth) to estimate actual session duration independently of last_activity.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Use the visitors table session_id + last_activity (no auth debounce) joined to users.id to compute a true post-signup engagement duration.

findings.title: Apple-relay "zero-action ghoster" cluster is a statistical phantom; real root cause is post-signup paywall (`/pricing?onboarding=1`), not Apple Sign-In
findings.summary: The premise of the bug — "Apple OAuth callback completes but the JS observer never fires for privaterelay users" — does not match the prod data. The "20-of-33 Apple-relay ghosters" cluster is an artifact of two unrelated facts: (a) 91% of Apple sign-ups use privaterelay (so any Apple ghoster cluster is dominated by relay), and (b) the zero-action rate is essentially identical across providers — 22.5% Apple vs 23.1% Google vs 15.8% email over 30 days (n=326). There is no Apple-specific or relay-specific signal. The actual root cause for the "no follow-up authenticated request after signup-tx" pattern is mundane: the welcome-flow A/B flag `welcome_flow_B_skip_create` was `false` for the entire analyzed 7-day window (it was flipped to `true` at 2026-05-30 23:33 UTC, *after* the analysis), so every new sign-up landed on `/my/pricing?onboarding=1` — a hard PlanModal upsell rendered as the very first screen. The client successfully receives the response (refresh_token row exists for every ghoster, 1 per user), navigates to Pricing, paints the paywall, and the user closes the app. Only 3 of 29 ghosters made even one authenticated `/api/*` call beyond signup (and `/api/billing/status` is silently `.catch(()=>{})` so it wouldn't bump `last_activity` if anything failed). A secondary finding worth flagging: the Capacitor user-agent marker (`appendUserAgent: "Capacitor lovetta-ios"` from capacitor.config.json) is missing from 100% of the 862 users in the last 90 days, yet 83 RevenueCat subscriptions exist — so server-side native-vs-web telemetry is silently broken. The `device_type` column shows only `web-*` values; `classifyDevice` never sees `lovetta-ios` and never returns `'ios'`. That's a real bug but unrelated to ghosters.

hypotheses.hypothesis: ROOT CAUSE: post-signup destination is `/my/pricing?onboarding=1` which renders a hard PlanModal upsell as the first screen, and a meaningful fraction of OAuth signups bounce there. This is provider-agnostic — Apple and Google ghost at the same ~23% rate — and the welcome_flow_B mitigation that would auto-provision Lily + drop the user into a real chat was OFF for the entire analyzed window (flipped to ON 14 minutes before this investigation).
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Watch zero-action rate post-2026-05-30T23:33Z. With welcome_flow_B at 50%, the cohort that gets `/chat/<id>?firstSession=1` should ghost at a markedly lower rate than the A_control cohort that still hits Pricing. If both cohorts ghost equally, the paywall hypothesis is wrong.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Keep the welcome_flow_B variant at 100% (not 50%) so every signup gets Lily-as-first-screen, and remove `/pricing?onboarding=1` as the post-signup fallback — replace with a soft value-preview screen. Add Sentry breadcrumb on PlanModal close-without-subscribe to quantify. (Also: instrument client-side pixel fires on /me success so ghosters surface as 'tokens-stored-but-no-engagement' separately.)

hypotheses.hypothesis: The 'Apple privaterelay zero-action' cluster is purely a base-rate artifact: 91% of Apple signups use relay, and ~22.5% of all Apple signups ghost, so by arithmetic ~21% of all Apple signups will be 'relay + ghost.' This pattern looks like a privacy-relay-specific bug but isn't — Google ghosts at the same rate without any relay involvement.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Compute zero-action rate of non-relay Apple users (the 5 of 57 with @icloud.com / personal emails) vs relay Apple users over a 60-day window where n is large enough. If rates match within sampling error (binomial CI), the relay angle is dead.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Do not pursue a privaterelay-specific code fix. The auth flow is fine; the conversion problem is funnel-shaped.

hypotheses.hypothesis: SECONDARY BUG (unrelated to ghosters): capacitor.config.json's appendUserAgent='Capacitor lovetta-ios' is silently dropped on requests reaching the server — 0 of 862 users in 90d have any Capacitor token in their stored UA, yet 83 RevenueCat subscriptions exist. classifyDevice never returns 'ios'; device_type column shows only web-* values. Either Capacitor isn't mutating navigator.userAgent for axios/fetch from the WKWebView, OR the Apple Sign-In and RevenueCat purchase flows happen via SFSafariViewController where the appendUserAgent doesn't apply.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Run the native iOS build, open Safari Web Inspector against the WKWebView, log `navigator.userAgent` at app boot, then `console.log(api.defaults.headers)` before /api/auth/apple. If neither shows 'Capacitor lovetta-ios', the appendUserAgent isn't being honored at the WKWebView level on the remote URL (https://lovetta.ai/my/) — likely because Capacitor only mutates UA for locally-served webDir bundles, not for `server.url` remote pages. The capacitor docs note this caveat.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Send explicit `X-Lovetta-Client: ios-native` header from the iOS build (set it via `axios.defaults.headers.common` when `isCapacitor()`) and use that header server-side instead of relying on UA-suffix. Update classifyDevice to read both. Backfill: any RevenueCat-subbed user is by definition native iOS — set device_type='ios' for those rows.

hypotheses.hypothesis: ALTERNATIVE (low confidence) — refreshUser() race in AppleSignIn.jsx: after `localStorage.setItem(tokens)`, the awaited `refreshUser()` hits /api/auth/me, but if the access-token signing-key cache hasn't loaded yet OR if there's a clock-skew issue between server clock and JWT iat/exp, /me could 401 silently and clearStoredTokens() would run. The user would be authenticated server-side but immediately logged out client-side.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Check Sentry / logs for any /api/auth/me 401 spike correlated with /api/auth/apple 200 events in the same second. Also check if engaged users have api_consumption rows in the first 30 seconds; ghosters don't. If 401-then-clear were the culprit, you'd expect Sentry errors. Logs grep for 'apple error' returned empty.
hypotheses.fix_outline: If true: after refreshUser() in AppleSignIn.jsx, if user is still null, hard-refresh tokens via /api/auth/refresh once before giving up. But evidence is thin — same ghosting rate on Google web (which doesn't use refreshUser; it sets tokens then reloads to /chat or /pricing) suggests this isn't it.

hypotheses.hypothesis: ALTERNATIVE (low confidence) — Login.jsx's <AppleSignIn> has no onSuccess prop, so successful Apple sign-in from the Login page (vs Signup page) just calls refreshUser() and relies on PublicRoute's <Navigate to='/' replace />. On `/`, ProtectedRoute fires before AuthContext propagates — could briefly redirect to /welcome in Capacitor, where user might bounce. But this is a tiny minority path (most signups go through /signup, not /login), and Apple Sign-In on Login.jsx without ageData would return 400 'age_consent_required' anyway and redirect to /signup.
hypotheses.how_to_falsify: Add console.log instrumentation showing which page mounted the AppleSignIn component when authorize was called. Or, in DB: signups landing on Login.jsx-route Apple would have failed first with age_consent_required (visible in any server log line). They didn't.
hypotheses.fix_outline: Pass `onSuccess` to <AppleSignIn> on Login.jsx that explicitly navigates to `/` after refreshUser resolves, instead of relying on PublicRoute redirect.

topHypotheses.source: Apple privaterelay "ghosters" are NOT an OAuth/token-persistence bug — they're native-iOS app users mis-tagged as web, abandoning the post-signup paywall
topHypotheses.hypothesis: Native iOS users land directly on a forced full-screen paywall (PlanModal fullScreen) after Apple Sign-In — they tap-out of the modal within ~1s before exploring the app. Compounded by the 'Skip for now' button being a small muted-text link at the bottom (PlanModal.jsx:291-295), users perceive a hard paywall and abandon to home screen.
topHypotheses.how_to_falsify: Add a `paywall_shown` user_event firing when PlanModal mounts with fullScreen=true and the user is brand-new (no companions). Then check the cohort: among new iOS-Apple ghosters do paywall_shown events exist? If yes, hypothesis confirmed. Also: temporarily remove the post-signup paywall for a 50% cohort and measure companion_created rate — expect the gap (38.5% → ~12.5% no-companion, matching the 'Apple/other' real-email row).
topHypotheses.fix_outline: Mirror what 2754226 did for the in-app paywall pop in CompanionList: defer the post-signup paywall until the user has engaged. Have native iOS Signup.jsx navigate to '/' (CompanionList) directly, and only show PlanModal after 3+ user messages. Same as the web 'onboarding=1' branch was modified to do.

topHypotheses.source: Apple privaterelay "ghosters" are NOT an OAuth/token-persistence bug — they're native-iOS app users mis-tagged as web, abandoning the post-signup paywall
topHypotheses.hypothesis: The capacitor `appendUserAgent: 'Capacitor lovetta-ios'` is not reaching the App Store binary. The deployed App Store IPA was built before the setting was added (or cap sync ios was never run + Xcode rebuild + resubmission). Result: server-side device classifier can't distinguish native iOS from web Safari → entire funnel analysis is misclassified. This is what made the user think 'web Apple users' exist when they don't.
topHypotheses.how_to_falsify: Open the live App Store build, run it on a real device, navigate to lovetta.ai/my, open Safari Web Inspector → check `navigator.userAgent`. If it doesn't include 'Capacitor lovetta-ios', the setting isn't applied. Alternatively, on the server log User-Agent of every /api/auth/apple call for 24h and check for the token.
topHypotheses.fix_outline: Rebuild iOS app with `npm run build:ios`, increment version, submit to App Store. ALSO add a more robust client hint: have AppleSignIn.jsx and api.js axios interceptor send `X-Lovetta-Platform: ios-native` header when isCapacitor() && getPlatform()==='ios'. Update server/src/device.js to read this header in addition to the UA. This decouples server analytics from the WKWebView UA quirks.

topHypotheses.source: Apple privaterelay "ghosters" are NOT an OAuth/token-persistence bug — they're native-iOS app users mis-tagged as web, abandoning the post-signup paywall
topHypotheses.hypothesis: The `last_activity` debounce in auth-middleware.js (60s window) makes the entire 'active_seconds = 0' signal in the weekly analysis a false positive. Users who DID make 5-10 authenticated calls in the first minute show active_seconds≈0 because only the first call (the one that wrote last_activity inside /api/auth/apple) registers, and subsequent calls hit the debounce.
topHypotheses.how_to_falsify: The Heroku router logs for shhrdvsh72 (IP 174.179.164.121) at 20:29:16-20:29:23Z already prove this — 5 protected API calls succeeded but DB shows active_seconds=0.328. Replace `last_activity` with a real 'first followup activity' metric: a counter on the JWT verify path that increments without debounce.
topHypotheses.fix_outline: Remove or shrink the ACTIVITY_DEBOUNCE to 5 seconds for the first 5 minutes after user creation. Or add a separate `activity_count` int column updated on every authenticated request. The weekly analysis's 'ghoster' definition should use 'no /api/auth/me after the signup transaction' (queryable from access logs) rather than DB-derived last_activity.

topHypotheses.source: Apple privaterelay "ghosters" are NOT an OAuth/token-persistence bug — they're native-iOS app users mis-tagged as web, abandoning the post-signup paywall
topHypotheses.hypothesis: Welcome Flow B (auto-provision a Lily companion at signup) is intended to fix exactly this — bypass the companion-create wizard so users land in chat directly. The feature is shipped (commit bece69d) and the flag is ON, but no users have signed up since 2026-05-30 23:33 UTC. Once new signups occur, if the flow works, the ghoster rate should halve.
topHypotheses.how_to_falsify: Manually create a test signup post-flag and check `user_companions.auto_provisioned=TRUE` exists and `welcome_flow_assigned` event fires. Bucket 50/50 means user IDs with hash<50 get B variant — verify hash distribution.
topHypotheses.fix_outline: Once new signups roll in, monitor the cohort: expected 50% get B_skip_create variant → land on /chat/<id>?firstSession=1 directly. If the auto-provisioned cohort still ghosts at the same rate, the bug is downstream of auth (chat page bugs), not at the paywall.

topHypotheses.source: Apple Sign-In ghost-signups: tokens minted server-side then nuked client-side by refreshUser's overzealous catch block
topHypotheses.hypothesis: ROOT CAUSE: After /api/auth/apple succeeds server-side, the client either never receives the response (iOS app backgrounded/suspended during/after the Apple sheet) OR receives it but the subsequent `await refreshUser()` in AppleSignIn.jsx fails at the network layer (DNS/TCP/CORS) — and AuthContext.refreshUser's catch block UNCONDITIONALLY clears both lovetta-token and lovetta-refresh-token even for transient network errors. The user is left logged out with a perfectly valid server-side account they can never re-enter without re-signing-in.
topHypotheses.how_to_falsify: Instrument AppleSignIn.jsx to logEvent('apple_post_succeeded') BEFORE localStorage.setItem and 'apple_refreshuser_succeeded' AFTER refreshUser. Compare counts to user-row count over 48h. If apple_post_succeeded < user-row count, response delivery is the issue. If apple_post_succeeded ≈ user-row count but apple_refreshuser_succeeded is 60% lower, refreshUser is the killer.
topHypotheses.fix_outline: (1) In AuthContext.refreshUser, DO NOT remove tokens on caught errors — only on a 401 from /me (let api.js interceptor handle the refresh+401 path). On network/5xx errors, keep tokens and rely on next user-initiated request. (2) In AppleSignIn.jsx, set user state directly from the apple response (skip refreshUser) and fall back to refreshUser only if data.user missing. (3) Add an apple-recovery flow: if localStorage has a refresh-token but no access-token, call /api/auth/refresh on app boot to rehydrate the session.

topHypotheses.source: Apple Sign-In ghost-signups: tokens minted server-side then nuked client-side by refreshUser's overzealous catch block
topHypotheses.hypothesis: Secondary contributor: Login.jsx mounts AppleSignIn WITHOUT an `onSuccess` prop. So when Apple succeeds on the Login screen, navigation depends entirely on user state propagating through React state → PublicRoute Navigate. If refreshUser fails (per primary hypothesis), no navigation happens, no error is shown (because the API succeeded), and the user is stranded. Signup.jsx wires onSuccess={handleSocialSuccess} which calls navigate(...) — but Signup-flow users still hit the refreshUser-clears-tokens bug.
topHypotheses.how_to_falsify: Check Apple sign-ins that came from /my/login vs /my/signup; if ghost rate is dramatically higher for Login.jsx (no onSuccess), this is a real factor. The current login UI is the only entry point for returning Apple users (Signup is for new), so the bulk of Apple Sign-Ins go through Login.jsx.
topHypotheses.fix_outline: Add explicit onSuccess={(data) => navigate('/')} to <AppleSignIn> in Login.jsx so the navigation is deterministic even if user state propagation is racey. Mirror for GoogleSignIn in Login.jsx.

verifications.hypothesis: Native iOS users land directly on a forced full-screen paywall (PlanModal fullScreen) after Apple Sign-In — they tap-out of the modal within ~1s before exploring the app. Compounded by the 'Skip for now' button being a small muted-text link at the bottom (PlanModal.jsx:291-295), users perceive a hard paywall and abandon to home screen.
verifications.refuting_evidence: Two framing issues weaken the hypothesis without refuting the mechanism: (1) The bug is NOT Apple-specific — Apple zero-action rate is 19.3% vs Google 28.0% vs Email 11.1% (7d, n=100). Apple dominates only by VOLUME (57/100 signups). 'No companion created' rate is nearly identical across providers (36.8%/36.0%/33.3%), pointing at a downstream-of-OAuth bottleneck affecting everyone equally. (2) Median time-to-first-api for engaged users is ~60-100s, not ~1s — users either spent real time on the paywall or were AFK. We can't confirm 'taps out within 1s' from data. (3) device_type='web-mobile' for the 'native iOS' cohort is a red-herring: 83 RevenueCat subscribers exist in DB (only possible from the native iOS app) yet ZERO users have 'Capacitor' or 'lovetta-ios' in their stored user_agent — meaning `appendUserAgent: 'Capacitor lovetta-ios'` in web/capacitor.config.json is not making it to req.get('User-Agent') on the server. So we can't actually distinguish native-iOS-app users from iOS Safari users in current telemetry. The 11 zero-action Apple privaterelay users plausibly are native-iOS-app users (privaterelay strongly implies native flow since AppleSignIn.jsx web-renders null), but we can't prove it. (4) The 'no /api/auth/me hit' framing from the signal was misleading — api_consumption table only tracks AI/billing calls, not /me. So we can't say what auth-context requests they made.
verifications.supporting_evidence: The mechanism is confirmed in code: Signup.jsx:178 calls `resolvePostSignupPath(signupResponse) || postSignupPath`. postSignupNav.js:11-17 ALWAYS returns '/pricing?onboarding=1' (when not in welcome_flow_B variant). Pricing.jsx:74-88 — when `?onboarding=1`, renders `<PlanModal isOpen fullScreen>` with `onClose={() => { localStorage.setItem('lovetta-plan-skipped','1'); navigate('/') }}`. Pricing.jsx:128-137 — even without `?onboarding=1`, `isAppStore()` (= isCapacitor()) ALSO renders `<PlanModal fullScreen>`. So BOTH native iOS and web users land on a forced full-screen paywall right after Apple/Google/email signup. PlanModal.jsx:291-295 — the only escape is `<button onClick={onClose} className="w-full py-3 text-brand-muted text-sm">Skip for now</button>` — small muted-grey text-button at the bottom, no border/affordance. - /Users/vasily/projects/lovetta/web/ios/App/App/capacitor.config.json line 7: same

Capacitor 8 sets applicationNameForUserAgent at CAPBridgeViewController.makeWebViewConfiguration(); when server.url is a REMOTE URL, the resulting UA is a WKWebView UA — but iOS WebKit does emit the applicationNameForUserAgent string when set before first load. The fact that it never appears in 6+ weeks of production traffic — including for paying RevenueCat users — strongly suggests the App Store binary was either built before the setting was effective, or `cap sync ios` was never run + Xcode rebuilt and the live IPA carries a stale Capacitor.plist. (I could not inspect the live IPA from this environment.)
verifications.refuting_evidence:
Important nuance — the hypothesis is correct as far as it goes, but it does NOT fully explain the "60 of 97 ghosters, 33 zero-action, 19 of 20 privaterelay had no /api/auth/me hit" original finding:

A) "Never made any subsequent /api/auth/me hit" is OVERSTATED. Heroku live logs show that real privaterelay Apple signups (e.g. shhrdvsh72@privaterelay.appleid.com signed up via /api/auth/apple at 2026-05-30T20:29:16) DO call GET /api/auth/me at +0.3s and GET /api/billing/status, /api/companions at +7s, +23s — all 200. The DB still shows last_activity = created_at + 0.3s for that user.

B) The reason for (A) is server/src/auth-middleware.js — updateActivity() debounces writes to users.last_activity for 60 seconds per user. Any authed request within the first 60s of signup does NOT bump last_activity. So "last_activity = created_at to the second" is a FALSE GHOSTER SIGNAL for any user whose entire session was < 60s — a normal pattern for mobile users who open, peek, close. The original analysis using delta < N seconds is biased by middleware debouncing, not necessarily by client-side auth failure.

C) The original report's use of api_consumption as proxy for "did user make any subsequent request" is wrong. server/src/consumption.js only inserts api_consumption rows for LLM/billable calls (chat, image, voice). A user who hit /api/auth/me, /api/billing/status, /api/companions but never sent a chat message would still show api_calls = 0.

D) The hypothesis's second-order causal chain ("misclassification is what made the user think 'web Apple users' exist when they don't") is therefore only partly right: it does explain the device-label confusion, but it does NOT explain the underlying ghoster cohort, which is itself partly a measurement artifact of the 60s debounce + LLM-only consumption logging.

E) Could not independently verify what UA the actual live App Store IPA sends without running on a real device.

verifications.hypothesis: The last_activity debounce in auth-middleware.js (60s window) makes the entire 'active_seconds = 0' signal in the weekly analysis a false positive.
verifications.refuting_evidence:
The hypothesis correctly identifies that auth-middleware.js has a 60-second activity debounce (lines 8-27 of /Users/vasily/projects/lovetta/server/src/auth-middleware.js — `ACTIVITY_DEBOUNCE = 60_000`), and DB evidence does show that mechanism corrupting last_activity for some users. But the strong claim that "the entire active_seconds=0 signal is a false positive" is REFUTED by independent server-side signals.

Bucketed all 109 users created in the last 7 days by (last_activity - created_at):
- delta < 1s ("zero_action_lt_1s"): 31 users → only 3 (10%) have any independent evidence of a followup call (companion_created event, api_consumption row, or first_message_sent). The other 28 (90%) have ZERO independent activity signals — no events beyond signup, no api_consumption, no companions, no conversations.
- delta 5-65s: 12 users → 6 with actions
- delta 65-130s: 5 users → 4 with actions  
- delta 130-250s: 13 users → 13 with actions
- delta > 250s: 48 users → 45 with actions

Crucially, the hypothesis's own example user `shhrdvsh72@privaterelay.appleid.com` (UUID 72266e0b-cf43-47f8-b7e7-beb22e62b56d) has DB last_activity = T+0.328s AND zero companions, zero messages, zero api_consumption rows, and only one event (signup). Heroku router logs do show 5 protected API calls (`/api/auth/me`, `/api/billing/status`, `/api/companions`, `/api/support/unread`, second `/api/billing/status`) between 20:29:16.355 and 20:29:23.682 — confirming the debounce/race corrupted his last_activity. But shhrdvsh72 also never reached companion-create, never sent a message. He IS a ghoster on the deeper signals.

The hypothesis's claim is that the analyst built the cohort solely from last_activity. But the cohort can be (and largely is) corroborated by independent server-side artifacts that the debounce cannot affect: user_events.event_type IN ('companion_created','first_message_sent') and api_consumption.user_id rows. For the privaterelay 7-day cohort: 16 zero-action users (delta<1s), of which 14 have no companion_created and no first_message_sent event at all. These are real drop-offs, not measurement artifacts.

Additional finding: the hypothesis's explanation of the mechanism is also slightly wrong. The activityCache is empty for a brand-new userId, so the FIRST middleware-call-after-signup should write through (no debounce hit). The actual cause of the < 1s delta for users WITH proven followups (`7gydrj928y` has companion_created at signup+34s but last_activity at signup+0.123s) is more likely a RACE between (a) the fire-and-forget `UPDATE users SET last_activity = NOW()` queued from /api/auth/apple (line 923) and (b) the fire-and-forget UPDATE queued by the middleware on the first followup call — both target the same row, neither awaits, and NOW() is evaluated per-statement at execution time, so the /apple UPDATE can land second and overwrite a later value back down. This is a separate bug from the 60-second debounce.

Net: the debounce/race definitely garbles last_activity for some users, but the weekly "60 of 97 fresh signups never returned past 5 min, 33 zero-action, 20 privaterelay zero-action" signal cannot be dismissed as artifact — most of those users genuinely have no companion_created event, no messages, no api_consumption rows. The Apple Sign-In dropout investigation should continue, not be closed.
verifications.supporting_evidence:
What the hypothesis got right (and is worth fixing independently):
1. ACTIVITY_DEBOUNCE = 60_000 in /Users/vasily/projects/lovetta/server/src/auth-middleware.js is real and observable — multiple users have last_activity values clustered exactly at signup+60.258s, +60.275s, +60.349s, +60.803s, +120.207s, +120.239s, +180.293s — the classic step-pattern of a 60s debounce.
2. User `7y8vct27bp@privaterelay.appleid.com`: signup at 16:09:24, companion_created at 16:10:15 (+51s), first_message_sent at 16:10:31 (+66s) — yet last_activity = 16:10:25 (+60.26s, truncated mid-session). Proves debounce is undercounting active users.
3. User `7gydrj928y@privaterelay.appleid.com`: last_activity = signup + 0.123s, but companion_created event at signup + 34.4s — proves at least SOME zero-action signal is false-positive.
4. `last_activity > created_at + INTERVAL '5 minutes'` is the EXACT formula in admin-api.js line 472 that drives the "returned" metric in the weekly analysis — so this debounce/race directly biases the headline number downward.

Recommended fix (independent of whether the hypothesis is the root cause of the dropout signal):
- Replace fire-and-forget `pool.query('UPDATE users SET last_activity = NOW() ...')` in /api/auth/apple line 923 (and the equivalent in google callback / google token / telegram, lines 224, 534, 673, 1039 of auth-api.js) with a write that doesn't race the middleware. Either await it before res.json(), or skip it entirely (let the next middleware call cover it).
- Add a monotonic guard: `UPDATE users SET last_activity = GREATEST(last_activity, NOW())` so fire-and-forget updates can never rewind the value.
- For analytics, replace the `last_activity > created_at + INTERVAL '5 minutes'` "returned" definition with a count of distinct user_events rows OR an EXISTS check against api_consumption / messages — robust signals that don't rely on the debounced column.
- Optionally shorten ACTIVITY_DEBOUNCE to 5-10s during the first 5 minutes of a user's life, or add an `activity_count` int column incremented without debounce.

verifications.refuting_evidence:
The hypothesis can't be refuted by data today because there are zero post-flag signups (only 22 minutes elapsed). However, the hypothesis presupposes that the root cause of ghosting is the post-signup multi-step UI funnel (Pricing → CompanionList → CompanionCreate). The actual prod evidence on privaterelay zero-action ghosters is inconsistent with this presupposition for the worst subset:

- 11 of 16 zero-action privaterelay ghosters in the last 7d have active_secs < 0.2 seconds AND api_calls = 0 (queried directly against api_consumption joined to users). Examples:
  • 1f686a8e: 0.07s, 0 api calls, 0 companions
  • d867eea3: 0.08s, 0 api calls, 0 companions
  • fac5388c: 0.09s, 0 api calls, 0 companions
  • p6gnj8fmj9: 0.06s, 0 api calls, 0 companions
  • fctt4z7zzs: 0.08s, 0 api calls, 0 companions
  • vc5rtrg665: 0.14s, 0 api calls, 0 companions

These users completed the /api/auth/apple POST (server-side state exists) but the client never made ANY subsequent authenticated request. Welcome Flow B inserts a companion + conversation + opener into the DB during the same signup transaction, but it does NOT change what the CLIENT does after the response. If the client is failing to persist the token, or the JS context is being destroyed when ASWebAuthenticationSession returns, or refreshUser() in AuthContext fails silently — Welcome Flow B doesn't help. The user still never loads /chat/<id>, because they never load any authenticated route.

For this subset, the hypothesis's "should halve" prediction is unlikely to play out. Auto-provisioning helps only users who DO navigate after auth but get filtered out at Pricing/CompanionList/CompanionCreate. For the harder subset (sub-second activity, zero subsequent API calls), the bug is upstream of any post-signup navigation logic — likely in token storage on the native bridge or the AppleSignIn.jsx callback never completing on the React side. The original task description's three possibilities (localStorage miss / cookie drop / native bridge race / JS observer never fires) are still in play, and the deployed fix does not address them.

So while the hypothesis isn't outright refuted (verification requires post-flag data), the suggested fix outline ("if auto-provisioned cohort still ghosts, the bug is downstream — chat page bugs, not paywall") misses the more likely third option: the bug is UPSTREAM of any navigation, at the auth-callback / token-persistence boundary on iOS native. A cleaner experimental design would also instrument /api/auth/apple with a server-issued sentinel cookie/header and confirm that a follow-up /api/auth/me hit arrives within N seconds — that would isolate "client never returned" from "client returned but bounced off Pricing".

verifications.hypothesis: ROOT CAUSE: After /api/auth/apple succeeds server-side, the client either never receives the response (iOS app backgrounded/suspended during/after the Apple sheet) OR receives it but the subsequent `await refreshUser()` in AppleSignIn.jsx fails at the network layer (DNS/TCP/CORS) — and AuthContext.refreshUser's catch block UNCONDITIONALLY clears both lovetta-token and lovetta-refresh-token even for transient network errors. The user is left logged out with a perfectly valid server-side account they can never re-enter without re-signing-in.
verifications.refuting_evidence:
Independent investigation refutes the hypothesis on three load-bearing points.

1) WRONG SURFACE — these signups are mobile-Safari, not iOS Capacitor.
Every privaterelay zero-action user in the 7-day cohort has device_type='web-mobile'/'web-tablet' and user_agent 'Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 18_x...) AppleWebKit/605.1.15... Mobile/15E148' with NO 'Capacitor' or 'lovetta-ios' token. classifyDevice() in server/src/device.js returns 'ios' for any Capacitor UA (line 13), so these rows are guaranteed to be Mobile Safari, not the iOS native app. AppleSignIn.jsx is gated to `if (!isCapacitor()) return null` (line 23) and never renders for these users. The hypothesis specifically blames AppleSignIn.jsx + Capacitor bridge races + ASWebAuthenticationSession, none of which are in scope here.

(Side note: there is no web Apple JS SDK in this repo — no AppleID.auth, no appleid.apple.com script include in public/*.html or web/src. How these Mobile Safari users reach POST /api/auth/apple is a separate question — likely the iOS Safari built-in 'Sign in with Apple' offered via WebAuthn/passkey UI calling /api/auth/apple by some path I did not locate. But it is definitely NOT the Capacitor path the hypothesis targets.)

2) RESPONSE IS DELIVERED — clients ARE making follow-up authenticated requests.
For user 72266e0b-cf43-47f8-b7e7-beb22e62b56d (shhrdvsh72@privaterelay.appleid.com) the prod heroku router log shows:
  20:29:16.034 POST /api/auth/apple → 200 (224ms)
  20:29:16.355 GET  /api/auth/me   → 200
  20:29:16.414 GET  /api/billing/status → 200
  20:29:23.680 GET  /api/companions → 200
  20:29:23.682 GET  /api/support/unread → 200
Tokens were persisted, attached as Bearer, and the protected /me round-tripped successfully. There is no evidence the client `await refreshUser()` failed or that tokens were cleared.

3) THE 'ZERO-ACTION' SIGNAL IS A MEASUREMENT ARTIFACT of the 60-second activity debounce.
server/src/auth-middleware.js lines 9-15: updateActivity() in-memory-caches per-user writes and SKIPS the UPDATE for 60s after the first hit. /api/auth/apple writes last_activity=NOW() at T=0 (auth-api.js line 923). The very next /api/auth/me runs `authenticate` middleware → updateActivity at T+~300ms → also writes (cache was empty). EVERY subsequent /companions, /billing/status, /support/unread call within 60s hits the debounce and is SKIPPED. So last_activity is permanently pinned to T+300ms unless the user keeps the app open >60s.

Smoking-gun cohort query (last 7d, signups, by auth_provider × email_type):
  apple/relay : total=52 sub1s=16 sub60s=16 past60s=36
  google/real : total=25 sub1s=8  sub60s=8  past60s=17
  email/real  : total=18 sub1s=4  sub60s=4  past60s=14
  apple/real  : total=5  sub1s=1  sub60s=1  past60s=4
sub1s exactly equals sub60s for ALL providers — no users have last_activity between 1s and 60s. That is mathematically impossible from real usage and is dispositive proof of the debounce artifact, not a Capacitor-flow bug. Apple-relay zero-action rate 31% (16/52) vs non-Apple 28% (12/43) — barely distinguishable, not the '55% ghoster' anomaly originally claimed.

4) THE UNCONDITIONAL-TOKEN-CLEAR FOOTGUN IS REAL BUT UNRELATED.
AuthContext.refreshUser (web/src/contexts/AuthContext.jsx lines 27-44) does clear both lovetta-token and lovetta-refresh-token on ANY caught error from /api/auth/me — including network failures — which IS a real bug that would log users out on transient outages on RETURN visits. The hypothesis correctly identifies the code defect. But it is not what is producing this signal: the signal users never get to a RETURN visit. Their first session's /me already returned 200, and they then closed the tab. Fixing the catch block would not change these users' behavior.

TRUE root cause for the engagement gap: these users complete sign-up, the SPA renders the empty post-auth home (no companions, no welcome content for the non-B onboarding variant), and they bounce within 5–10 seconds without creating anything. The activity-debounce makes them look like 'zero-action' in the DB metric. The real product problem is empty-state onboarding for new Apple-relay users, not an auth/token bug.
verifications.supporting_evidence:

There is no 401 vs network-error discrimination; transient failures (DNS, offline, 5xx) wipe the session. This is a real latent bug worth fixing per the suggested 'do not clear on non-401' remediation.
- AppleSignIn.jsx flow does await refreshUser() AFTER token persistence (line 67). A network blip between localStorage.setItem and the /me roundtrip would orphan the user as described — but I found zero evidence this is occurring for the affected cohort; their /me calls returned 200.

verifications.refuting_evidence:
Why I'm downgrading confidence to LOW even though the code asymmetry is real:

1. Signup.jsx's onSuccess wouldn't actually rescue the user from the primary refreshUser-clears-tokens bug. `handleSocialSuccess` calls `navigate(resolvePostSignupPath(data) || '/pricing')`. All destinations are wrapped in `<ProtectedRoute>` (App.jsx:42-47), which checks `user` from AuthContext. If refreshUser already wiped tokens and set user=null, ProtectedRoute redirects to `/welcome` (Capacitor) or `/login`. So adding `onSuccess={(data) => navigate('/')}` to Login.jsx doesn't materially change outcomes — the user ends up at /login either way once user state is null. The proposed fix is cosmetic w.r.t. the primary bug.

2. Cannot falsify from prod data which page the ghosters came from. No `signup_origin_page` or referrer in user_events.metadata for the signup event; UTM fields are null for the Apple cohort. The hypothesis's how-to-falsify cannot be executed.

3. The "Login.jsx is the only entry for returning Apple users" claim is questionable: an apple_id already-existing user signing in via Apple does NOT fire a `signup` user_event (only first-time creation does in auth-api.js:869). So the 16 ghosters in the cohort, by definition, are all FIRST-TIME Apple users — for which `age_consent_required` is required. On Login.jsx, AppleSignIn is mounted without ageData and onboardingData was already cleared on prior signup. Server returns `age_consent_required` (auth-api.js:840), AppleSignIn.jsx catches it and does `window.location.href = '/my/signup'` (line 73-76) BEFORE any successful signup happens. So new Apple users CANNOT successfully signup directly from /login — they get hard-redirected to /signup, where they go through the age gate and then hit AppleSignIn with onSuccess wired. So the *successful* first-Apple-signup path goes through Signup.jsx, NOT Login.jsx — which means the hypothesis's premise that ghosters came primarily from Login.jsx is likely WRONG for the new-user cohort.

4. Therefore the Login.jsx missing-onSuccess gap is unlikely to be the cause of these specific 16 ghosters. The signal points back to the PRIMARY hypothesis (refreshUser clears tokens silently on transient /api/auth/me failure) regardless of which page they came from.

Summary: the code-level observation is true, but the causal claim ("this contributes to the privaterelay zero-action ghost rate") is poorly supported because (a) new Apple users get redirected from Login → Signup before they can complete on Login, and (b) the proposed fix doesn't escape ProtectedRoute when user=null.
